subject: Company Law
lesson_title: Section 158 and the Division of Powers

questions.question: Omega Ltd’s shareholders pass an ordinary resolution at a general meeting instructing the board of directors to sell the company’s main factory to a buyer the shareholders have identified. The board refuses. Under section 158 CA 2014 and established case law, who prevails?

options.A: The shareholders prevail — a majority of members can direct the board by ordinary resolution on any business matter
options.B: The board prevails — under S158, management of the business is vested in the directors; members cannot direct the board by ordinary resolution on matters within the board’s remit
options.C: The shareholders prevail, but only if they hold at least 75% of the shares
options.D: The board must comply if the shareholders represent a majority in value of share capital

questions.explanation: Section 158 CA 2014 vests management of the company’s business in the directors. Per John Shaw & Sons (Salford) Ltd v Shaw [1935] and Automatic Self-Cleansing Filter Syndicate v Cuninghame [1906], the board exercises its powers in its own right — members cannot direct the board by ordinary resolution on matters within the board’s constitutional remit. Members’ only recourse is to amend the constitution (special resolution) or remove directors (S146).

questions.question: The board of Ryanair attempted to veto the proposed takeover of Aer Lingus without holding a shareholder vote. In Ryanair DAC v Aer Lingus Group DAC [2012], the court held that:

options.A: The board had absolute discretion to block any merger or acquisition without member consultation
options.B: The board’s authority under S158 is not unlimited — certain fundamental decisions (including mergers) must be referred to the general meeting as a matter of law or constitution
options.C: The board’s decision was an internal management matter and the court had no jurisdiction to intervene
options.D: The takeover was automatically void as no special resolution had been passed

questions.explanation: Ryanair DAC v Aer Lingus Group DAC [2012] illustrates that while S158 vests broad management authority in the board, certain strategic decisions can require shareholder approval — either by the constitution or by statute (e.g., fundamental changes). The case is an important qualification to the board’s S158 authority.

questions.question: A director purports to enter a contract on behalf of the company, but exceeds the authority given to him by the board. A third party who acted in good faith seeks to enforce the contract. Under section 40 CA 2014, which of the following best describes the third party’s position?

options.A: The contract is void — a director cannot bind the company beyond actual authority
options.B: The contract is enforceable against the company — S40 protects third parties dealing in good faith from limitations on directors’ authority arising from the company’s constitution or internal procedures
options.C: The contract is enforceable but only if the director had at least ostensible authority
options.D: The third party must first exhaust remedies against the director personally before suing the company

questions.explanation: Section 40 CA 2014 (implementing the EU First Directive) provides that a company cannot rely on restrictions in its constitution to deny a contract entered into by a director with a third party acting in good faith. S40 removes the doctrine of constructive notice of the constitution insofar as it limits authority. The company is bound even if the director exceeded internal limits.

questions.question: In John Shaw & Sons (Salford) Ltd v Shaw [1935], the Court of Appeal confirmed that the board exercises its management powers:

options.A: As delegates of the general meeting — the board derives authority from the shareholders
options.B: In its own right, not as agents or delegates of the shareholders — the board’s authority flows from the constitution, not from the general meeting
options.C: Subject to the right of majority shareholders to override board decisions at any time by ordinary resolution
options.D: Only within the objects of the company as set out in the constitution

questions.explanation: In John Shaw & Sons (Salford) Ltd v Shaw [1935] 2 KB 113, the Court of Appeal confirmed that the directors exercise their powers in their own right — they are not agents of the company acting on behalf of shareholders. The authority of the board flows from the constitution (and S158 CA 2014), not from any delegation by the general meeting.

questions.question: Apex Ltd’s constitution provides that any contract over €100,000 requires the prior approval of the audit committee. The managing director enters a €250,000 contract with a supplier without the audit committee’s approval. The supplier did not know about the committee requirement. Can the company deny liability on the contract?

options.A: Yes — the audit committee requirement is in the constitution, which is a public document and the supplier is deemed to have notice of it
options.B: No — section 40 CA 2014 removes constructive notice of constitutional restrictions on directors’ authority; the company is bound if the supplier acted in good faith
options.C: Yes — a contract exceeding the value approved by the board is automatically void under S228 CA 2014
options.D: No — but only if the managing director had apparent authority, which requires actual knowledge by the company of the managing director’s authority

questions.explanation: Section 40 CA 2014 removes constructive notice of constitutional restrictions on directors’ powers. The supplier is not required to have read the constitution to find the audit committee requirement. As long as the supplier acted in good faith (and there is no evidence they knew the director was exceeding their authority), the contract is enforceable against the company.

questions.question: Under the CA 2014, can the general meeting effectively reassert control over the board’s management powers without removing the directors?

options.A: Yes — by passing an ordinary resolution directing the board on any specific matter
options.B: No — the only mechanisms are: (i) amend the constitution by special resolution to restrict board powers, or (ii) remove directors under S146 and appoint more compliant directors
options.C: Yes — by passing a special resolution directing the board on any specific matter
options.D: Yes — a unanimous written resolution of all shareholders overrides any board decision

questions.explanation: Following John Shaw, Automatic Self-Cleansing Filter, and S158 CA 2014, the general meeting cannot interfere with the board’s management powers by ordinary resolution. The members’ remedies are: (i) amend the constitution by special resolution (75%) to change the scope of board authority; or (ii) remove directors under S146 and appoint new ones. A directing ordinary resolution is ineffective.

questions.question: What is the legal effect of the board being deadlocked (e.g., two directors with equal votes who cannot agree) on an important business matter?

options.A: The company is automatically put into examinership if the deadlock persists for more than 30 days
options.B: In extreme cases, the general meeting may be entitled to act in place of a deadlocked board under the principle in Marshall’s Valve Gear Co v Manning Wardle [1909]
options.C: The company secretary has a casting vote in the event of board deadlock
options.D: The board deadlock is automatically resolved in favour of the chairperson’s preferred option

questions.explanation: Marshall’s Valve Gear Co v Manning Wardle [1909] 1 Ch 267 recognised that in exceptional circumstances — where the board is deadlocked and the company cannot function — the general meeting may be able to act in place of the board. This is a narrow exception to the S158 principle and requires a genuine deadlock preventing the company from operating.

questions.question: A director acts outside the board’s authority but the act is subsequently ratified by the board. However, a minority shareholder argues the ratification itself was invalid because the same director who acted without authority voted in the ratification resolution. Under general company law, the correct position is:

options.A: The ratification is automatically invalid — a director cannot vote on their own ratification
options.B: The ratification is valid if the director who acted had no personal financial interest in the ratification; a director interested in a matter must disclose but may still vote unless the constitution prohibits it
options.C: The ratification requires a special resolution to be effective
options.D: The ratification requires court approval where a director votes on their own actions

questions.explanation: A director with a conflict of interest must disclose it under S231 CA 2014. Whether an interested director can vote on the ratification depends on the constitution — many constitutions restrict interested directors from voting. Without such a restriction, the interested director may vote (subject to conflict disclosure obligations). However, where the ratification itself amounts to a breach of duty, shareholder (not board) ratification may be required.

questions.question: Section 158 CA 2014 provides that unless the constitution otherwise provides, the management of the company’s business is vested in the directors. What is the most significant practical implication of the phrase ‘unless the constitution otherwise provides’?

options.A: The court can always override the constitution if justice requires
options.B: Shareholders can, by special resolution, amend the constitution to reserve specific decisions for the general meeting, thereby limiting the board’s authority in those areas
options.C: The CEA can issue directions to the board overriding the constitution
options.D: The constitution can grant the board unlimited authority to trade beyond the company’s legal capacity

questions.explanation: The phrase ‘unless the constitution otherwise provides’ is critical: it means that shareholder control over board authority is not entirely excluded. By amending the constitution via special resolution (75%), members can reserve specific categories of decision (e.g., disposal of major assets, borrowing over a threshold) for member approval, thereby limiting the board’s S158 authority in defined areas.

questions.question: In Automatic Self-Cleansing Filter Syndicate v Cuninghame [1906], the Court of Appeal held that the board of directors:

options.A: Must comply with shareholder directions on all matters given by ordinary resolution
options.B: Acts under the authority of the articles and is not a mere agent of the shareholders — a majority resolution cannot compel the board to sell company property contrary to the board’s decision
options.C: Has authority derived solely from the shareholders and must carry out whatever the majority decides
options.D: Must refer all decisions involving assets worth over £500 to the general meeting

questions.explanation: Automatic Self-Cleansing Filter Syndicate v Cuninghame [1906] 2 Ch 34 established the cornerstone of board authority: the directors act under the authority of the articles and are not mere agents of the shareholders. The court refused to order the board to execute a contract for the sale of the company’s property that the majority of shareholders wanted but the board opposed.

questions.question: A company’s constitution provides that the board may not borrow more than €500,000 without shareholder approval. The MD borrows €750,000 from a bank without obtaining approval. The company later seeks to avoid the loan. Under section 40 CA 2014, the bank:

options.A: Must repay the money as it was put on notice of the borrowing limit by the constitution
options.B: Can enforce the loan against the company — S40 protects a third party (the bank) dealing in good faith; the constitutional limit on borrowing does not bind the bank unless the bank had actual notice of the restriction
options.C: Can enforce the loan only if the MD had actual authority
options.D: Cannot enforce the loan because borrowing limits are in the constitution, which is a public document and the bank is deemed to have notice of it

questions.explanation: Section 40 CA 2014 removes the old constructive notice doctrine. A third party dealing in good faith with a director is not expected to read and analyse the company’s constitution for internal limits on authority. The bank can enforce the loan unless it had actual knowledge of the borrowing limit restriction. The company is bound.

questions.question: The Quin and Axtens v Salmon [1909] decision is significant for which of the following propositions?

options.A: Shareholders can alter the articles by ordinary resolution to remove directors at any time
options.B: A provision in the articles protecting a specific director’s veto over certain transactions is enforceable even against a majority shareholder seeking to override it
options.C: The constitution can validly exclude all shareholder rights to vote on board decisions
options.D: The board must hold a general meeting before entering any contract above £10,000

questions.explanation: Quin and Axtens v Salmon [1909] AC 442 confirmed that constitutional provisions protecting individual directors’ rights (such as veto rights over certain transactions) are enforceable. A majority of shareholders cannot override such a provision by ordinary resolution — they would need to amend the constitution by special resolution, which itself requires 75% support.

questions.question: What is the minimum number of directors required to constitute a quorum at a board meeting of an LTD with four directors, where the constitution is silent on quorum?
questions.explanation: The default quorum for board meetings under the Model Constitution is two directors. Where the constitution is silent, this default applies. A meeting purporting to transact business below quorum is invalid and any resolutions passed at it may be challenged.

questions.question: A director is appointed to the board by the shareholders to represent a particular class of shareholders. At a board meeting, the director votes against the interests of the company (and in the interests of his appointing shareholders). This behaviour is:

options.A: Permissible — the director is entitled to represent the interests of those who appointed them
options.B: A breach of the director’s fiduciary duty under S228(1)(a) CA 2014 — a director must act in the interests of the company as a whole, not in the sectional interests of the appointing shareholders
options.C: Permissible if the constitution expressly permits it
options.D: Permissible if the director discloses their conflict of interest before the vote

questions.explanation: Section 228(1)(a) CA 2014 requires every director to act in good faith in the interests of the company. A nominee director owes their primary duty to the company, not to their appointing shareholder. Voting in the interests of the appointing shareholder against the interests of the company breaches S228(1)(a) — even if the person was appointed by that shareholder.

questions.question: The Bushell v Faith clause (weighted voting on director removal resolutions) was upheld by the House of Lords in the UK in 1970. Under Irish law, such a clause is:

options.A: Clearly valid — Bushell v Faith [1970] is binding in Ireland
options.B: Clearly void — Irish courts have expressly invalidated weighted voting clauses
options.C: Of uncertain validity in Ireland — the better view is that S146 CA 2014 cannot be circumvented by a constitutional weighted voting clause, though this has not been definitively resolved by the courts
options.D: Valid but only if it was included in the constitution at the time of incorporation

questions.explanation: Bushell v Faith [1970] AC 1099 is an English House of Lords decision — it is persuasive but not binding in Ireland. The better view in Irish academic commentary and practice is that a Bushell v Faith clause would likely be void in Ireland as inconsistent with the intent of S146 CA 2014, which provides a fundamental right of members to remove directors by ordinary resolution. However, this has not been definitively decided by the Irish superior courts.

questions.question: A company’s constitution states that no contract for services above €200,000 may be entered into without approval by two-thirds of the board. The MD, one of three directors, signs a €300,000 services contract alone. Assuming the third party had no knowledge of the two-thirds requirement, which of the following is correct?

options.A: The contract is void — internal board approval requirements are part of the company’s constitution and bind all parties
options.B: The contract is voidable at the company’s option — the company may ratify it by a two-thirds board vote
options.C: The contract is enforceable against the company under S40 CA 2014 — the third party was not required to verify compliance with internal board approval procedures
options.D: The MD is personally liable on the contract as they exceeded authority

questions.explanation: Section 40 CA 2014 protects third parties dealing in good faith from the company’s internal procedural requirements. An internal requirement for two-thirds board approval does not bind the third party who had no knowledge of it. The company is bound by the contract. The MD may be personally liable to the company for breach of duty but that is a separate matter.

questions.question: Section 158 CA 2014 is described as the ‘cornerstone of corporate governance.’ Which of the following best explains why?

options.A: It gives shareholders the final say on all business decisions
options.B: It clearly delineates the division of powers between the board and the general meeting — vesting ongoing management in the directors while reserving constitutional changes and fundamental decisions for shareholders
options.C: It prohibits directors from acting without a specific shareholder mandate for each decision
options.D: It grants each director an individual power to bind the company by contract

questions.explanation: Section 158 CA 2014 is the cornerstone of corporate governance because it establishes the fundamental division of powers: day-to-day management is vested in the board, while shareholders exercise control through constitutional amendment, appointment and removal of directors, and approval of specified fundamental decisions. This two-tier structure provides governance clarity and operational efficiency.
